document.title: Cúram 8.1.2 Income Support for Medical Assistance Long Term Care Guide

root.title: Cúram 8.1.2 Income Support for Medical Assistance Long Term Care Guide
root.summary: Overview of the Cúram 8.1.2 Income Support for Medical Assistance Long Term Care guide. It explains the business purpose and audience, then outlines Long Term Care (LTC) Medical Assistance as three coverage types: LTC, Medically Needy LTC, and Medically Needy LTC with Spend Down. The guide covers eligibility fundamentals, institutional or HCBS waiver paths, certification and financial unit rules, resource/income tests, invalid resource transfer sanctions, spousal impoverishment, post-eligibility cost of care, and expensive-home rules. It also describes eligibility determination and product delivery creation, plus change-in-circumstance reassessment for income, expense, household, and resource changes. The section includes the table of contents and ends with legal notices, privacy, and trademark/copyright information.

children.title: Note
children.summary: Introductory note advising readers to review the Notices section on page 37 before using the information or the product it supports. Includes Merative US L.P. copyright dates (2012, 2024).

children.title: Edition
Edition — pages 5-14
children.summary: Overview of the Long Term Care Medical Assistance guide and its Cúram 8.1/8.1.1/8.1.2 edition notes. Covers purpose, audience, supporting docs, and a table of contents. Introduces LTC program structure: three coverage types, eligibility criteria, waivers, post-eligibility cost-of-care, transfer-of-resources rules, spousal impoverishment, and expensive home rules. Also explains major Cúram Express Rules changes, including removal of activation-time sanction processing and new eligibility-decision display of invalid transfers and spousal details.

children.title: Edition — pages 15-24
children.summary: Covers Long Term Care Medicaid eligibility and rules: eligibility decisions, creating LTC product delivery, and detailed program criteria. Key topics include institutionalization, level of care, 30-consecutive-day rule and exceptions, categorical eligibility, expensive home test, waiver groups, transfer-of-assets penalties, resource rules for different financial units, spousal impoverishment assessments, and income options (LTC, Medically Needy LTC, and Spend Down). Useful for checking core LTC qualification, sanctions, and how income/resources are evaluated.

children.title: Edition — pages 25-34
children.summary: Covers Long Term Care income eligibility and post-eligibility cost-of-care calculations. Includes rules for institutionalized blind/disabled children, married couples, single institutionalized individuals, and medically needy LTC with and without spend down. Details income tests, deeming, deductions, and allowances: earned income, personal needs, community spouse, family dependent, medical expense, home maintenance, and guardian fees. Also explains change-in-circumstance reassessment rules and how evidence changes affect eligibility and cost-sharing.

children.title: Edition — pages 35-36
children.summary: This section explains how changes trigger reassessment for Long Term Care eligibility and cost of care. It covers income changes for the institutionalized individual, spouse, and other household members; expense changes affecting allowable deductions and post-eligibility cost of care; and resource changes that trigger resource eligibility rules. It notes that excess resources can make an individual ineligible from the following month, and that spousal impoverishment cases require monitoring of resource changes against protected amounts.

Overview of the Long Term Care Medical Assistance guide for Cúram 8.1–8.1.2. Explains purpose, audience, related docs, and LTC program structure: three coverage types, eligibility criteria, waivers, resource/income tests, transfer-of-assets penalties, spousal impoverishment, post-eligibility cost of care, and expensive-home rules. Also notes Cúram Express Rules changes, including removed activation-time sanction processing and improved eligibility-decision display for invalid transfers and spouse details. Includes change-in-circumstance reassessment rules for income, expenses, and resources.

children.title: Notices
Privacy policy
children.summary: This section provides legal notices: sample data disclaimer, copyright/license terms for sample programs, warranty/liability limits, a link to Merative’s privacy policy, and trademark acknowledgments for Merative, IBM, Adobe, Oracle, Linux, Microsoft, and UNIX. It is useful if you need licensing, usage rights, or branding/legal reference details.

children.title: Trademarks
children.summary: This section is the document’s legal/trademark notice. It states that example names are fictitious, sample programs are provided “AS IS” under the Merative Client Agreement, and references the Merative privacy policy. It lists trademarks for Merative, IBM, Adobe, Oracle/Java, Linux, Microsoft/Windows, and UNIX, and notes that other names may be trademarks of others. Copyright is © Merative US L.P. 2012, 2024.

Legal notices and use terms for the publication: personal and internal enterprise copying are allowed only if proprietary notices are preserved; distribution, display, or derivative works outside those limits need Merative consent. It disclaims warranties, limits rights and licenses, notes export-law compliance, and states Merative may withdraw permission. It also covers third-party product/source disclaimers, non-endorsement of external websites, patent notice, and the copyright © Merative US L.P. 2012, 2024.

children.title: Contents
children.summary: Table of contents for the “Income Support for Medical Assistance Long Term Care Guide.” Lists main sections on introduction, LTC Medical Assistance overview, eligibility determination and product delivery, LTC rules, and additional processing. Also enumerates subtopics such as certification, financial unit, resources, income limits, institutionalization, level of care, waivers, transfer of assets, and change-in-circumstance handling, with page references for navigation.

children.title: 1 Income Support for Medical Assistance Long Term Care Guide
1.1 Introduction
Document Purpose
children.summary: Explains the purpose of the Long Term Care Medical Assistance Guide: a business-level overview of the LTC Medical Assistance program and its implementation in Cúram Income Support for Medical Assistance. Identifies the intended audience as anyone needing to understand the Cúram LTC implementation, including recorded evidence, evidence activation, and case processing. Also points readers to related Medical Assistance documentation and notes that the next section provides an overview of LTC, key differences from other coverage types, and Express Rules changes.

children.title: Audience
children.summary: Intended for anyone seeking a business-level understanding of Cúram’s Long Term Care Medical Assistance implementation. This section explains the document’s purpose, the audience, and where to find related Medical Assistance documentation. It frames LTC Medical Assistance as coverage for individuals needing extended institutional care, and indicates the guide covers program evidence, evidence activation, and LTC case processing. Useful as the entry point for readers deciding whether the guide matches their need for LTC program and system overview.

children.title: Available Documentation
children.summary: Lists supporting documentation for Long Term Care Medical Assistance. It points readers to the main Business Analysts guide, Cúram Income Support for Medical Assistance Program Guide, and notes that caseworkers may also use related Medical Assistance guides, including the Spend Down Guide and the Children’s Health Insurance Program Guide. Useful as a reference node for finding related program documentation rather than substantive program content.

Introduces the Long Term Care Medical Assistance guide as a business-level overview of the LTC program and its Cúram Income Support implementation. It targets readers who need to understand LTC evidence, evidence activation, and case creation/management. The section also points to related Medical Assistance documentation, including the main program guide, Spend Down, and Children’s Health Insurance Program guides. Useful as the entry point for deciding whether the document covers the LTC program and system processes you need.

children.title: 1.2 Long Term Care Medical Assistance Overview
Introduction
children.summary: Introduces the Long Term Care Medical Assistance guide and its purpose: a business-level overview of the LTC program and its Cúram implementation. It targets readers seeking to understand the program, required evidence, evidence activation, and LTC case processing. It also points to related documentation for Medical Assistance, Spend Down, and CHIP. The section begins the LTC overview, including differences from other coverage types and changes introduced with Cúram Express Rules.

children.title: About Long Term Care Medical Assistance
children.summary: Introduces Cúram Long Term Care Medical Assistance, covering institutional-level care for people needing long-term medical, social, personal, or supportive services. Explains that LTC may be provided in institutions or through HCBS waiver programs and that “institutionalized individual” includes both settings. Lists core eligibility requirements: institutionalization, non-financial, level of care, 30-day requirement, categorical eligibility, no expensive home, no invalid transfer sanction, resource test, and income test. Also outlines three LTC coverage types and their different income tests.

children.title: Differences with Other Coverage Types
Coverage Types
children.summary: This section explains Long Term Care Medical Assistance and its three coverage types: Long Term Care, Medically Needy Long Term Care, and Medically Needy Long Term Care with Spend Down. It outlines what LTC means, who may qualify (institutionalized individuals or HCBS waiver recipients), and the key eligibility requirements such as level of care, resource and income tests, categorical eligibility, and transfer rules. It also highlights how LTC differs from other coverage types, including post-eligibility cost of care and spousal impoverishment assessment.

children.title: Waivers
children.summary: Explains Long Term Care “Waivers” eligibility within the coverage types. Describes five pass paths—institutedized and four waivers (Elderly, Physical Disabilities, Mental Retardation/Developmental Disabilities, Brain Injury)—with only one needed and results shown on the Household tab. Notes waiver eligibility evidence (disability, level of care, medical institution, age). Also covers post-eligibility cost of care, transfer-of-resources look-back rules, spousal impoverishment resource assessment, and key changes under Cúram Express Rules.

children.title: Post Eligible Cost of Care Calculation
children.summary: Explains post-eligibility cost-of-care for Long Term Care, where institutionalized individuals usually contribute from monthly income after deductions. Also covers transfer-of-resources assessment with a 60-month look-back period, and spousal impoverishment resource assessment, which lets a community spouse retain some resources within a protected period. Notes LTC coverage types and waiver pathways, plus Cúram Express Rules changes such as three LTC coverage types, removal of special evidence-activation processing, and invalid transfers creating ineligibility at application.

children.title: Transfer of Resources Assessment
children.summary: Explains Long Term Care resource-transfer rules. Covers the 60-month look-back period, requiring applicants to report resources transferred by themselves, a spouse, or a representative to determine if transfers were made to qualify for LTC and trigger ineligibility. Also describes spousal impoverishment resource assessment for community spouses, including protected-period transfers and assessment date rules. Notes Cúram Express Rules changes: three LTC coverage types, removal of special evidence-activation processing, and invalid transfers now create ineligibility at application and appear in eligibility decisions.

children.title: Spousal Impoverishment Resource Assessment
children.summary: Explains spousal impoverishment rules for Long Term Care Medical Assistance when an institutionalized person has a community spouse. Covers the assessment date, protected period, and how resources can be transferred to the spouse so the institutionalized individual can meet resource limits. Also notes related LTC context: waiver eligibility, post-eligibility cost-of-care, transfer-of-resources look-back rules, and Cúram Express Rules changes such as three LTC coverage types and invalid resource transfers causing ineligibility at application.

Explains how Long Term Care Medical Assistance differs from other coverage types. It notes LTC covers people needing an institutional level of care for an extended period, including institutional settings and HCBS waivers, and lists the core eligibility requirements. It also highlights the main distinctions: three LTC coverage types with different income tests, multiple eligibility pathways, post-eligibility cost-of-care rules, invalid transfer penalties/sanctions, and spousal impoverishment resource assessment.

children.title: Changes to LTC Coverage Types with Cúram Express Rules
Special Processing at Evidence Activation
children.summary: Explains changes to Long Term Care eligibility processing at evidence activation: prior asset-transfer sanctioning was removed and replaced by eligibility-rule-based invalid resource transfers, allowing sanctions/periods of ineligibility to be adjusted. Notes that invalid resource transfers appear in LTC compliance decisions, with periods of ineligibility for applications and sanctions for ongoing cases. Also covers spousal impoverishment details now shown in eligibility decisions, and expensive home rules based on a $525,000 equity limit. Begins the eligibility determination/product delivery chapter.

children.title: Invalid Resource Transfers
children.summary: Explains changes to LTC eligibility processing for invalid resource transfers. Instead of only creating sanctions, the system now creates a period of ineligibility at application and a sanction for ongoing LTC cases; both make the individual ineligible. Invalid resource transfer decisions are driven by transfer-to-individual evidence and shown in the Compliance section of eligibility decisions. The section also notes related eligibility displays for spousal impoverishment and expensive home rules, and that special evidence-activation processing for sanctions has been removed.

children.title: Spousal Impoverishment
children.summary: This section explains Long Term Care eligibility decision changes for spousal impoverishment and related resource rules. It notes that spousal impoverishment details now appear in eligibility decisions on reassessment, rather than after product delivery activation. It also covers removed special processing at evidence activation, new invalid resource transfer handling, and expensive home rules, including the $525,000 equity limit and exceptions when certain individuals live in the home.

children.title: Expensive Home
children.summary: Explains LTC eligibility decision behavior for expensive home rules. An “expensive home” is an equity interest in a home above the state limit of $525,000, unless certain qualifying individuals live there. Also notes related eligibility decision changes: spousal impoverishment details now appear in eligibility decisions, invalid resource transfers and sanctions are handled by rules, and these items display in household/compliance eligibility views.

This section outlines major LTC coverage type changes under Cúram Express Rules: there are now three LTC coverage types, special processing at evidence activation was removed, and invalid resource transfers now create a period of ineligibility at application and appear in eligibility decisions. It also summarizes LTC waiver eligibility, post-eligible cost of care, transfer-of-resources assessment, and spousal impoverishment resource rules, including the 60-month look-back period and protected period.

Overview of Long Term Care (LTC) Medical Assistance and its Cúram implementation. Defines LTC as coverage for institutional-level care, including HCBS waivers, and lists eligibility basics: institutionalization, non-financial and level-of-care tests, 30-day rule, categorical eligibility, no expensive home, no invalid transfer sanction, resource and income tests. Describes three coverage types with different income tests, waiver pathways, post-eligibility cost-of-care, transfer-of-resources look-back (60 months), spousal impoverishment rules, and Cúram Express Rules changes such as removal of special evidence activation processing.

children.title: 1.3 Eligibility Determination and Product Delivery Creation
Introduction
children.summary: Introduces Long Term Care eligibility changes and chapter scope. It explains that spousal impoverishment and expensive home details now appear in eligibility decisions, and that special processing at evidence activation has been removed in favor of eligibility-rule handling of invalid resource transfers. It distinguishes invalid resource transfers as periods of ineligibility for applications versus sanctions for ongoing LTC cases. It also begins the eligibility determination/product delivery chapter and states certification requirements for institutionalized individuals under standard medical assistance coverage types.

children.title: Certification Requirements
children.summary: This section defines Long Term Care certification requirements. It states that institutionalized individuals who are eligible for or receiving standard medical assistance under existing coverage types meet categorical eligibility for nursing home or home-and-community-based care. It also sits within eligibility determination and product delivery creation, with nearby topics covering invalid resource transfers, spousal impoverishment, and expensive home rules shown in eligibility decisions. Open this node for the basic LTC certification criterion and related eligibility context.

children.title: Financial Unit
children.summary: Explains how Long Term Care applicants are assigned a financial unit for income and resource processing: single institutionalized individual, blind/disabled child, married couple with institutionalized spouse, or married couple with community spouse. Covers resource declaration, resource limits, deemed parental resources for blind/disabled children, separate treatment of couples, and spousal impoverishment rules for institutionalized individuals with community spouses (post-Oct 1989), including assessment date, community spouse share, and resource limit comparison.

children.title: Declaration of Resources
children.summary: Explains how Long Term Care Medical Assistance evaluates resources. Applicants must declare resources to determine eligibility, possible uncompensated transfers, and whether a community spouse exists. Covers resource limits for single individuals, blind/disabled children, married couples, and institutionalized spouses, including deeming of parental resources and joint counting for couples. Describes pre- and post-Oct 1989 spousal impoverishment rules, assessment date, community spouse share, and how resources are compared to LTC limits.

children.title: Resource Limits
children.summary: Explains LTC Medical Assistance resource eligibility. All applicants must pass a resource test; countable resources determine whether limits are met. Covers special rules for blind/disabled institutionalized children, single institutionalized individuals, married couples, and institutionalized spouses with community spouses. Describes how resources are counted for couples and how pre-/post-October 1989 institutionalization affects spousal impoverishment processing. Introduces the assessment date, community spouse share, and how resource limits are compared, including the usual $2,000 limit.

children.title: Invalid Resource Transfers
children.summary: Explains how invalid resource transfers affect Long Term Care Medical Assistance eligibility: if an invalid transfer exists or is found within the look-back period, a period of ineligibility or sanction is created and the applicant remains ineligible until it ends. Also covers the income test and how individual contributions are calculated from gross monthly income minus specific deductions/allowances. Describes the eligibility cascade for LTC coverage types, financial unit determination, post-eligibility deductions, and protected period rules for required resource transfers.

children.title: Income Limits
children.summary: Explains Long Term Care Medical Assistance income limits and how income affects eligibility. Covers invalid resource transfers and resulting periods of ineligibility, monthly individual contributions based on gross income minus specific deductions/allowances, and how the system checks LTC coverage types in the eligibility cascade. Also describes resource unit identification, required nonfinancial/categorical/level-of-care checks, post-eligibility deductions, and protected period rules for required resource transfers.

children.title: Individual Contributions
children.summary: Explains how Long Term Care Medical Assistance eligibility is evaluated, focusing on invalid resource transfers, income limits, and the calculation of an individual’s monthly contribution toward cost of care. Describes allowable deductions/allowances used in that contribution calculation and notes that the result appears in the eligibility decision. Also outlines how LTC coverage is checked within the broader Medical Assistance cascade, including financial unit selection, non-financial and level-of-care requirements, post-eligibility deductions, and protected period rules for required resource transfers.

children.title: Check Eligibility for Medical Assistance Programs including Long Term Care
children.summary: This section explains how Medical Assistance eligibility is checked for Long Term Care coverage. It covers invalid resource transfers and resulting periods of ineligibility, income limits and individual contribution calculations, and the coverage cascade for LTC, Medically Needy LTC, and Spend Down. It also describes the financial resource unit selection, required nonfinancial and categorical tests, level of care and 30-day requirements, post-eligibility deductions, and protected period rules for transferring assets.

children.title: View Eligibility Decisions for Medical Assistance Programs including Long Term Care
children.summary: Explains how Medical Assistance eligibility decisions display for Long Term Care. After checking rules, clients may be eligible for one of three LTC coverage types, with detailed income, resource, spousal impoverishment, and cost-of-care information shown, plus ineligible decisions. Also covers when caseworkers create LTC product delivery cases, including impacts on other household members and potential asset-transfer effects on spouses. Useful for understanding LTC eligibility results and authorization implications.

children.title: Create Long Term Care Medical Assistance Product Delivery
children.summary: Explains how caseworkers create Long Term Care Medical Assistance product delivery for eligible household members. Covers eligibility outcomes for LTC, Medically Needy LTC, and Spend Down, and what eligibility decision details display, including cost of care, income deductions, and spousal impoverishment/resource assessment results. Notes that LTC choices can affect other household members’ benefits (for example LIFC termination or community spouse eligibility) and that authorization assumes the client accepts conditions such as cost of care and any required asset transfer.

Covers LTC eligibility and product delivery basics: certification requirements for institutionalized individuals, financial unit classification, resource declaration and limits, invalid resource transfers, income limits, individual cost-of-care contributions, eligibility cascade, protected periods, eligibility decision displays, and creating LTC product delivery cases. Notes key rule changes: spousal impoverishment and expensive home details now appear in eligibility decisions, and invalid transfers are handled by eligibility rules rather than evidence-activation processing. Includes special rules for community spouses, institutionalized couples, and Medically Needy LTC/spend down.

children.title: 1.4 Long Term Care Rules
Introduction
children.summary: Introduces the Long Term Care rules chapter for determining LTC eligibility. It states that eligibility requires institutionalization, meeting nonfinancial requirements, level of care, 30 consecutive day rules, categorical eligibility, no expensive home, no invalid resource transfer sanction, and passing resource and applicable income tests. Also outlines key definitions for institutionalized status, level of care certification/validity, standard citizenship/SSN/residency requirements, and the 30-day institutional or waiver-based residence condition.

children.title: Institutionalized
children.summary: Defines the long-term care eligibility rules for institutionalized individuals. It states that LTC requires a Medical Institution record plus satisfaction of nonfinancial requirements, level of care, 30 consecutive day rule, categorical eligibility, resource and income tests, and no expensive home or invalid resource transfer sanction. It also specifies level-of-care criteria and certification, and lists the 30-day institutional settings: Title XIX nursing facility, hospital inpatient, or HCBS waiver participation.

children.title: Level of Care
children.summary: Defines Long Term Care eligibility rules. Covers the required conditions for LTC qualification, including institutionalization, non-financial requirements, level of care, 30 consecutive day rule, categorical eligibility, no expensive home, no invalid resource transfer sanction, resource test, and applicable income test. The level of care subsection specifies acceptable care levels (intermediate/skilled nursing, intermediate care for the mentally retarded, hospital level, or IMD), plus physician/recognized authority certification and valid LOC certification.

children.title: Non Financial Requirements
children.summary: This section states that a child must meet the standard non-financial eligibility rules for Citizenship, SSN, and Residency to qualify for Long Term Care. It points to the Common Rules chapter for the full details. The surrounding LTC eligibility context also notes institutionalization, level of care certification, and a 30-consecutive-day institutional requirement.

children.title: 30-Consecutive Day Requirement
Exceptions to the 30-Consecutive Day Requirement
Disabled Child
children.summary: Explains the “Disabled Child” exception to the 30-consecutive-day institutional stay rule for Medicaid long-term care. A disabled child in a medical institution is treated as a resident if the stay is expected to exceed 30 days, even if temporary. An infant born disabled and kept in the hospital is considered a resident from birth. The section also briefly restates 30-day rule exceptions, special absences, categorical eligibility, and ABD special conditions for age, blindness, disability, and deemed SSI recipients.

Lists exceptions to the 30-consecutive-day institutional stay rule for Medicaid LTC. Key exceptions include being Medicaid-eligible on entry, dying before 30 days, and a disabled child whose stay is expected to exceed 30 days. It explains disabled-child treatment in hospitals, including infants born disabled and kept in the hospital, who are considered residents from birth. Also notes special 30-day rule handling for brief absences and HCBS transitions, plus brief criteria for categorical eligibility and ABD special conditions.

children.title: Special Considerations for 30 day rule
children.summary: Explains exceptions and special handling for the 30-consecutive-day rule in long-term care/medical assistance. Key points: the rule is waived if the person already had Medicaid on entry, dies before 30 days, or is a disabled child expected to stay over 30 days. It also treats certain newborns/infants as residents from birth. The rule still counts if absent up to 14 consecutive days under institutional jurisdiction, or when moving from a medical facility to HCBS within 10 days of nursing home discharge. Includes categorical eligibility and ABD-related qualifying conditions.

Defines the 30-consecutive-day institutional requirement for Medicaid LTC eligibility. An individual must spend at least 30 consecutive days in a Title XIX certified nursing facility, as a hospital inpatient, or in an HCBS waiver program, unless an exception applies. This section is the core rule statement and points readers to exceptions handled elsewhere.

children.title: Categorically Eligible
ABD Special Conditions
children.summary: Explains ABD special conditions for Medicaid long-term care eligibility. Covers exceptions to the 30-consecutive-day rule, including prior Medicaid receipt, death before 30 days, and disabled children in medical institutions. Notes when temporary absences or rapid HCBS waiver entry still satisfy the rule. Defines categorical eligibility and outlines ABD LTC qualifying pathways: age 65+, SSA/DDS-certified blind or disabled status, or deemed SSI recipient status under Protected SSI.

Defines categorical eligibility for Medicaid LTC. A person may qualify by current Medicaid coverage (with some exclusions), by meeting Aged/Blind/Disabled criteria, or through certain waivers. Also outlines ABD LTC pathways: age 65+, SSA/DDS-certified blindness or disability, or deemed SSI recipient status under Protected SSI. Includes 30-day rule exceptions and special cases for hospital/medical institution residents, disabled children, temporary absences up to 14 days, and rapid HCBS waiver entry within 10 days of nursing home discharge.

children.title: Expensive Home
children.summary: Explains the “Expensive Home” Medicaid/LTC rule: an LTC individual cannot own a home above the $525,000 equity limit unless an exception applies (spouse lives in the home, or a child under 21 lives there, or a blind/totally disabled child lives there). Also lists waiver eligibility groups and core criteria for Brain Injury, MR/DD, Physical Disability, and Elderly waivers, including age, disability certification, and required level of care.

children.title: Waivers
Brain Injury
children.summary: Eligibility rules for the Brain Injury waiver. Applicants must be age 18 to under 65, have a certified traumatic or acquired brain injury, not be a TBI exception, and qualify for Nursing Home Level of Care. The page also notes an “expensive home” rule for LTC individuals and lists other waiver groups available in the program: MR/DD, Physical Disability, and Elderly.

children.title: MR/DD
children.summary: MR/DD waiver eligibility requires mental retardation evident before age 18 or developmental disability evident before age 22, plus qualification for ICF/MR level of care and certification as developmentally disabled or mentally retarded. The section also sits within broader waiver criteria that include brain injury, physical disability, and elderly groups, and it notes an “expensive home” resource limit exception for LTC individuals based on spouse or qualifying child occupancy.

children.title: Physical Disability
children.summary: This section outlines eligibility rules for LTC waiver groups, especially Physical Disability. For the physical disability waiver, applicants must be at least 18, qualify for Nursing Home Level of Care, and have a medically certified physical disability. It also briefly covers an “expensive home” exception tied to home equity limit $525,000 and family living situations, plus other waiver groups: Brain Injury, MR/DD, and Elderly, each with age, certification, and level-of-care requirements.

children.title: Elderly
children.summary: This section defines eligibility for the Elderly waiver and related LTC rules. It states the applicant must be at least age 65 and qualify for Nursing Home Level of Care. It also includes an “expensive home” rule: the LTC individual cannot own a home above the home equity limit ($525,000) unless a spouse lives there, or a child under 21 lives there, or a child who is blind or totally/permanently disabled lives there.

Waivers lists the LTC waiver eligibility pathways: Brain Injury, MR/DD, Physical Disability, and Elderly. It includes an “expensive home” exception for LTC individuals with home equity over $525,000, allowing exceptions if a spouse or qualifying child lives in the home. Each waiver has age, disability/certification, and level-of-care requirements: Brain Injury (18–64, traumatic/acquired brain injury, no TBI exception, nursing home LOC), MR/DD (onset before 18/22, ICF/MR LOC), Physical Disability (18+, medically certified disability, nursing home LOC), and Elderly (65+, nursing home LOC).

children.title: Transfer of Assets
Existing Eligibility Period
Invalid Resource Period Determination
Look-Back Period
children.summary: Explains the Medicaid LTC asset transfer look-back period and related penalty rules. For transfers on or after Feb. 8, 2006, the look-back date is 60 months before the later of the institutionalization date or Medicaid application date. The section links look-back review to transfers for less than fair market value, uncompensated value, ineligibility/sanction periods, existing eligibility periods, and undue hardship criteria. Includes a note on determining which household members’ transfers to individuals must be reviewed.

Explains how to determine an invalid resource transfer period for LTC Medicaid. Covers the transfer-of-assets penalty process for less than fair market value transfers, including checking existing ineligibility periods, uncompensated value, sanction start/end dates, and undue hardship exceptions. For transfers on or after Feb. 8, 2006, the look-back date is 60 months before the later of institutionalization or Medicaid application. Also notes how to identify household members whose transfers to an individual must be reviewed.

Explains how to check whether an existing LTC Medicaid ineligibility/sanction period is already in effect on application. If the period has expired, then review for invalid resource transfers; if it has not expired, the household is ineligible. Also introduces undue hardship criteria, the 60-month look-back date for transfers on or after Feb. 8, 2006, and the need to identify which household members’ transfers must be reviewed for penalties.

children.title: Penalty for Resources Transferred by the following Individuals
Transfer of Resource Penalty Exception
Allowable Transfer
children.summary: Explains how to identify invalid resource transfers in the look-back period and assess penalties: check whether assets are countable, whether an exception applies, and whether uncompensated value is greater than zero. Lists who transfers are evaluated for (individual and spouse, or agents acting on their behalf). Details allowable transfers without penalty, including certain transfers to a spouse, blind/disabled children, trusts for them, specific home transfers to relatives/caregivers, proof Medicaid was not the reason, and pre-Feb. 8, 2006 trusts.

children.title: Medicaid not the Reason for Transfer
children.summary: Explains how to assess Medicaid resource transfer penalties in the look-back period: determine whether assets are countable, whether an exception applies, and whether uncompensated value remains. Lists who can trigger penalties (the individual and spouse, or anyone acting for them). Details allowed transfers without penalty, including transfers to a spouse, certain blind/disabled children, qualifying trusts, homes to children/siblings/caregivers, pre-2006 trusts, and proof Medicaid was not the reason. Defines that proof requires unexpected circumstances after the transfer.

Defines when to apply a resource transfer penalty and key exceptions. For countable assets transferred in the look-back period, check whether an exception applies; if not, calculate uncompensated value and assess if it is greater than zero. Penalties may involve transfers by the individual or spouse, or anyone acting for them. Exceptions include transfers to a spouse, certain blind/disabled children or trusts, specific home transfers to relatives/caregivers, pre-Feb. 8, 2006 trusts, and proof Medicaid was not the reason for transfer, supported by post-transfer unexpected circumstances.

children.title: Length of Penalty
children.summary: Explains Medicaid LTC penalty calculations for transferred assets: sanction months equal uncompensated transfer value divided by the nursing home private-pay rate, with no maximum penalty. Describes handling of partial months/days, penalty start date rules (institutionalized, Medicaid-applied, otherwise eligible; or first of next month if transfer occurs after eligibility), and that penalties run consecutively. Also introduces financial unit categories and basic resource rules for single institutionalized individuals and institutionalized blind/disabled children.

Explains how to identify invalid resource transfers in the look-back period for an individual or spouse (or anyone acting for them): determine whether assets are countable, whether an exception applies, then calculate uncompensated value and check if it is above zero. Lists penalty-free transfers, including transfers to a spouse, certain blind/disabled children or trusts, specific home transfers to relatives/caregivers, pre-Feb. 8, 2006 trusts, and cases proving Medicaid was not the reason for transfer due to unexpected post-transfer circumstances.

children.title: Penalty Period Start Date
children.summary: Explains when Medicaid LTC penalty periods start. The penalty begins when the person is institutionalized, has applied for Medicaid, and is otherwise eligible except for the transfer penalty; if the transfer happens after eligibility, the start date is the first day of the next month. Penalties run consecutively, not concurrently. The section also outlines how to calculate penalty length from uncompensated transfer value divided by the private-pay rate, plus brief notes on financial unit categories and resource rules.

Explains LTC Medicaid transfer-of-assets rules for determining ineligibility penalties. Covers checking any existing sanction period on application, the 60-month look-back for transfers on/after Feb. 8, 2006, undue hardship, and which individual/spouse transfers to review. Describes the decision flow: identify countable assets, exceptions, and uncompensated value. Lists penalty-free transfers, including to a spouse, certain blind/disabled children or trusts, qualifying home transfers to relatives/caregivers, pre-Feb. 8, 2006 trusts, and cases proving Medicaid was not the reason for transfer.

children.title: Financial Unit
children.summary: Explains Medicaid long-term care penalty calculations and when penalty periods start, then defines the four financial unit types used for LTC eligibility: disabled child, single institutionalized individual, married institutionalized couple, and institutionalized with community spouse. Summarizes resource rules for single institutionalized individuals (only their countable resources are used) and for blind/disabled children under 18, whose parent resources are deemed to the child. Useful for eligibility and resource-deeming decisions.

children.title: Resources Rules
Single Institutionalized Individual
children.summary: Explains Medicaid LTC penalty and resource rules for a single institutionalized individual. The penalty period is based on uncompensated transferred assets divided by the nursing home private-pay rate, with no maximum period; partial months are converted to days. Penalties start when the person is institutionalized, has applied for Medicaid, and would otherwise be eligible, or the first of the next month if transfer occurs after eligibility. For single institutionalized individuals, only the individual’s countable resources are considered against the resource limit.

children.title: Institutionalized Blind or Disabled Child
Disabled Child Exception
children.summary: Explains a disabled child resource exception for institutional Medicaid. If a disabled infant is born in a hospital and remains there through the month of birth, or a disabled infant remains hospitalized long after birth, no parental resources are deemed and the child is treated as institutionalized from birth/month of birth. The section also briefly covers resource deeming for an institutionalized disabled child under 21, married institutionalized spouses, and spousal impoverishment rules for community spouses.

Explains resource rules for an institutionalized blind or disabled child under 18. If an unemancipated blind/disabled child enters an institution after living with at least one parent, the parents’ resources are deemed to the child. This section sits within long-term care financial unit/resource rules and helps determine Medicaid eligibility for institutionalized disabled children.

children.title: Married couple both institutionalized (or on a waiver)
children.summary: Explains Medicaid resource rules for married couples when both spouses are institutionalized or on a waiver: count each spouse’s resources, combine them, and compare to the LTC couple resource limit. Also introduces spousal impoverishment for one institutionalized/waiver spouse and one community spouse, describing asset assessment rules. Includes an older pre-1/10/1989 rule (count only the client’s assets, including joint assets) and notes that post-1/10/1989 cases after 30 days use combined couple assets.

children.title: Married institutionalized individual with community spouse
Last Entry Pre 01/10/1989
children.summary: Rules for resource/asset eligibility in institutional and waiver Medicaid cases. Covers deeming parent resources to an un-emancipated blind/disabled child under 21 entering an institution, with exceptions for disabled infants in hospitals. Explains resource tests for married couples when both are institutionalized or on waiver, and spousal impoverishment rules when one spouse is institutionalized/waiver and the other is in the community. This specific “pre 01/10/1989” rule says only count assets in the client’s name, including joint assets, and compare to the asset limit.

children.title: Last entry on or post 01/10/1989
children.summary: Explains Medicaid resource rules for institutionalized and waiver clients. Covers deemed resources for an un-emancipated blind/disabled child in an institution, including a disabled child exception with no parental deeming. Notes resource calculations for married couples both institutionalized and for an institutionalized spouse with a community spouse under spousal impoverishment rules. For cases on or after 01/10/1989, if the client was married to the current spouse and institutionalized 30+ days, eligibility is based on the couple’s combined assets.

Medicaid resource rules for institutionalized and waiver cases with a community spouse. Covers spousal impoverishment asset assessment, including how to count and compare resources for married couples. For pre-01/10/1989 cases, only assets in the client’s name count, including joint assets. For 01/10/1989 or later, if married to the current spouse and institutionalized 30+ days, eligibility is based on combined couple assets. Also references child deeming rules and disabled infant exceptions, plus married couples where both are institutionalized or on waiver.

Covers Medicaid LTC resource rules by financial unit: single institutionalized individuals (count only their countable resources), institutionalized blind/disabled children (parental resources may be deemed; disability and age/school exceptions apply), married couples both institutionalized/waiver (combine spouses’ countable resources against the couple limit), and institutionalized individuals with a community spouse (spousal impoverishment asset assessment, including pre-01/10/1989 and post-01/10/1989 rules). Also includes the LTC resource limit and child/resource deeming exceptions.

children.title: Income Rules
Long Term Care Income Option
Gross Income Test
Institutionalized Blind or Disabled Child
children.summary: Rules for eligibility of an institutionalized blind or disabled child. If an unemancipated blind/disabled child under 18 lives with a parent, or under 21 and in school, enters an institution, parent resources or income are deemed to the child for the eligibility month, then child and parent income are calculated and compared to the state monthly income standard. Exceptions: a disabled baby born in a hospital and remaining there, an infant born disabled staying hospitalized, or an emancipated child are treated as single institutionalized individuals with no parental deeming. Also introduces Medically Needy LTC income option and gross income test.

Explains the LTC gross income test and related income levels. It covers Medically Needy LTC, Medically Needy LTC with spend down, and the Long Term Care Income Option (special income level test at less than 300% of SSI FBR). The section gives the income-eligibility steps, then details how to total countable gross unearned and earned income for: married couples where both are institutionalized, single institutionalized individuals, and married couples with one institutionalized spouse and one community spouse, compared to the state maximum monthly income standard.

Explains the Long Term Care Income Option, a special income level test for LTC programs using a threshold of less than 300% of the SSI federal benefit rate. It outlines the eligibility steps: identify the financial unit, apply the gross income test and deductions, and deny LTC if the gross income test is failed. It then details how to total countable gross earned and unearned income for married couples (both institutionalized or one community spouse) and single institutionalized individuals, compared to the state maximum monthly income standard.

children.title: Medically Needy Long Term Care Income Option
Gross Income Test
Institutionalized Blind or Disabled Child
children.summary: Explains income/resource treatment for an institutionalized blind or disabled child. If an unemancipated blind/disabled child under 18 enters an institution after living with a parent, parental resources are deemed; if under 21 and in school, parental income is deemed. The section outlines calculating the child’s unearned and earned income, parent income for deeming, adding deemed income to the child’s income, then comparing gross countable income to the facility’s monthly LTC Reimbursement Rate to determine eligibility or medical-needy ineligibility. Introduces a disabled child exception.

Explains the gross income test for LTC eligibility. Covers institutionalized individuals and married couples (both institutionalized or one in community), including how to total gross earned/unearned income, compare it to the facility’s monthly LTC Reimbursement Rate, and when deductions apply for medically needy cases. Includes the full step-by-step process for an institutionalized blind or disabled child, parent-to-child deeming, and disabled child exceptions (e.g., infant born/remaining in hospital, emancipated child). Useful for income-comparison and eligibility rules before post-eligibility deductions.

Explains Medically Needy LTC income eligibility. Covers determining the financial unit, running the gross income test, and comparing income to the facility’s monthly LTC Reimbursement Rate. If income exceeds the rate, apply deductions (earned income, PNA, community spouse, family dependent, medical expenses, home maintenance, legal guardian) to see if remaining income is eligible and to set cost-of-care contribution. Also includes special rules for married couples, single institutionalized individuals, and institutionalized blind/disabled children, including parent-to-child deeming and disabled child exceptions.

children.title: Medically Needy Long Term Care with Spend Down Income Option
Income Test
children.summary: Explains income eligibility rules for long-term care and institutionalized individuals. Covers the LTC gross income test (above 300% SSI), spend-down steps for Medically Needy LTC, and how to calculate net income for institutionalized blind/disabled children, married couples where both spouses are institutionalized, and single institutionalized individuals. Includes deeming rules from parents/spouses, income deductions, comparison to state LTC or Medically Needy Income Eligibility Standards, and exceptions for disabled newborns in hospitals and emancipated children.

Explains Medically Needy LTC with Spend Down income eligibility. Covers the gross income test (only when income is over 300% of SSI), the step-by-step spend-down process, and how to determine net income for institutionalized children, married couples where both spouses are institutionalized, and single institutionalized individuals. Includes parent/spouse deeming, income deductions, comparisons to state LTC or Medically Needy income standards, and exceptions where disabled newborns, infants remaining hospitalized, or emancipated children are treated as single institutionalized individuals.

children.title: Deductions
Earned Income Deduction
children.summary: This section only points readers to the separate “Earned Income Deductions” sub-section in the Adult - Common Income Rules chapter; it does not define the deduction here. The surrounding content notes that after all deductions and allowances, the remaining income is the individual’s monthly contribution toward cost of care. If you need the actual earned income deduction rules, open the referenced sub-section.

children.title: Personal Needs Allowance
children.summary: Explains how to calculate an individual’s personal needs allowance when determining monthly cost-of-care contribution. Covers nursing facility rules, including the standard Basic Personal Needs Allowance and a special $90 allowance for certain veterans or surviving spouses receiving a reduced VA pension. Also lists waiver-based allowances: 100% of the federal poverty rate for Elderly/Disabled, MR/DD, and Brain Injury waivers, and 300% of the SSI rate for Physical Disabilities waivers. Begins community spouse needs allowance rules.

children.title: Community Spouse Needs Allowance
children.summary: Explains how to calculate an institutionalized person’s contribution toward cost of care after income deductions, focusing on personal needs allowances and community spouse needs allowance. Covers nursing facility rules, the reduced VA pension exception ($90 personal needs allowance), and waiver-based allowances for Elderly/Disabled, MR/DD, Brain Injury, and Physical Disabilities waivers. Also notes that if the individual has a community spouse, the allowance amount is determined by court or fair hearing, or such a determination may not exist.

children.title: Family Dependent Allowance
children.summary: Explains how to calculate a Family Dependent Allowance for an institutionalized individual with dependent family members other than the community spouse. The allowance applies only if the dependents meet family-dependent criteria and the individual agrees to provide support. It distinguishes cases where dependents live with the community spouse versus elsewhere. This excerpt begins the calculation rules for dependents living with the spouse, following the broader rules for community spouse needs and income allocations.

children.title: Medical Expense Deduction
children.summary: Explains when medical expenses can be deducted from income for Medicaid/long-term care budgeting. A medical expense is deductible only if it is allowable, incurred by the individual, not already used for spend-down, not covered by third-party payment, and not already paid by Medicaid. Deduct the individual’s paid amount; prorate nonmonthly premiums to a monthly figure; for combined family premiums, deduct only the individual’s share. The page also continues family dependent allowance and home maintenance allowance rules.

children.title: Home Maintenance Allowance
children.summary: Explains when a Home Maintenance Allowance can be deducted: the individual is away from home, a doctor expects return within 6 months, and there is no community spouse living in the home. It also says to calculate the home’s shelter expenses, then set the allowance to the lesser of the shelter expenses or the SSI level for one individual. This section sits within broader rules on family dependent allowances and medical expense deductions.

children.title: Legal Guardian Expense Deduction
children.summary: Explains legal guardian expense deduction: if an individual has a guardian and the guardianship fee is at or below the limit, deduct it; if above the limit, cap it at the limit and deduct that amount. Also introduces additional Long Term Care processing for change-in-circumstance reassessment when evidence changes. Covers impacts of household and income changes on LTC eligibility and cost-of-care liability, including potential ineligibility from the following month if income exceeds limits.

Covers post-eligibility LTC Medicaid income deductions used to calculate monthly cost-of-care liability. The section outlines the deduction order: earned income deduction, personal needs allowance, community spouse needs allowance, family dependent allowance, medical expense deduction, home maintenance allowance, and legal guardian expense deduction. It gives rules for personal needs allowances in nursing facilities and waivers, community spouse allowance calculations and court-order cases, family dependent allowance methods, deductible medical expense criteria, and home maintenance allowance limits.

Covers LTC income eligibility and post-eligibility deductions. Explains the three income options: Long Term Care (<300% SSI FBR), Medically Needy LTC (income vs facility reimbursement rate, then deductions), and Medically Needy LTC with Spend Down (>300% SSI FBR). Details gross income tests for single, married, and parent-deeming/disabled child cases, plus special exceptions. Also outlines cost-of-care deductions in order: earned income, PNA, community spouse allowance, family dependent allowance, medical expenses, home maintenance, and legal guardian expenses.

Defines LTC Medicaid eligibility rules and income/post-eligibility calculations. Covers required conditions: institutionalization, citizenship/SSN/residency, level of care certification, 30-day institutional/waiver rule, categorical eligibility, expensive home limit ($525,000 equity with exceptions), and transfer-of-assets sanctions/look-back. Also outlines waiver pathways (Brain Injury, MR/DD, Physical Disability, Elderly), resource rules, and income methods for single, married, and waiver cases plus cost-of-care deductions (PNA, community spouse, dependents, medical, home maintenance, guardian).

children.title: 1.5 Additional Long Term Care Processing
Change in Circumstance Processing
children.summary: Explains how Long Term Care cases are reassessed when evidence changes on the Integrated Case. It describes change-in-circumstance processing, including reevaluating eligibility, cost of care liability, and transfers relevant to spousal impoverishment or improper post-eligibility resource transfers. It also outlines impacts of household changes and income changes: household additions/removals usually do not affect the institutionalized individual unless a spouse or dependent changes, while income changes trigger reassessment and may end eligibility the following month or recalculate cost of care.

children.title: Impact of Evidence Changes on Long Term Care Eligibility
Household Changes
Income Changes
children.summary: Explains how income changes affect Long Term Care eligibility after an institutionalized individual is already eligible. The person is treated as a single income unit and must remain under the LTC income limit. New or changed income evidence triggers reassessment: if over the limit, the individual becomes ineligible starting the first day of the following month; if still within the limit, post-eligibility rules rerun to recalculate cost-of-care contribution.

children.title: Expense Changes
children.summary: This section explains how expense changes affect Long Term Care reassessment for institutionalized individuals. Changes in the individual’s expenses can alter allowable income deductions and the post-eligibility cost of care, triggering income and cost-of-care rules. A spouse’s expense changes trigger cost-of-care redetermination. Expense changes for other household members do not affect LTC coverage, though they may affect cost-of-care liability.

children.title: Resource Changes
children.summary: Explains how income, expense, and resource changes trigger reassessment for institutionalized individuals in Long Term Care. Income and expense changes may update cost-of-care contributions through eligibility and post-eligibility rules; changes for spouses or family dependents can also affect cost of care, while other household members usually do not affect coverage. Resource changes trigger Resource Eligibility reruns, and exceeding the limit can make the person ineligible from the following month. Also includes Merative notices, permissions, warranty/disclaimer text, privacy policy, and trademarks.

Overview of change-in-circumstance processing for Long Term Care. Explains how evidence changes on an Integrated Case trigger reassessment of eligibility, cost-of-care liability, and spousal impoverishment/resource monitoring. Covers household changes, income changes, expense changes, and resource changes: institutionalized individuals are treated as a single income unit after eligibility, over-limit income/resources can end eligibility starting the first day of the following month, and spouse/dependent changes can affect cost of care while other household members usually do not. Includes Merative notices, privacy policy, and trademarks.

Explains how Integrated Case evidence changes trigger Long Term Care reassessment. Covers household, income, expense, and resource changes; institutionalized individuals are treated as a single income unit after eligibility. Over-limit income or resources can make the person ineligible starting the first day of the following month, while spouse/dependent changes can affect cost of care and other household members usually do not. Includes Merative legal notices, privacy policy, and trademarks.

Explains additional Long Term Care change-of-circumstance processing for Integrated Case evidence changes. Covers reassessment of eligibility, cost of care liability, and detection of improper resource transfers/spousal impoverishment issues. Summarizes how household, income, expense, and resource changes affect institutionalized individuals: spouse/dependent changes may alter cost of care, other household changes usually do not, and income/resource over limits can make the individual ineligible from the first day of the following month. Also includes Merative notices, privacy policy, and trademarks.

Overview of Long Term Care Medical Assistance in Cúram: the guide explains the program purpose, evidence and case processing, and related documentation. It covers LTC coverage types (Long Term Care, Medically Needy LTC, and Medically Needy LTC with Spend Down), eligibility basics, waiver pathways, post-eligibility cost-of-care, transfer-of-resources look-back, spousal impoverishment, and expensive home rules. It also summarizes eligibility determination, product delivery creation, and change-of-circumstance reassessment rules, then ends with notices, privacy, and trademarks.
